content.problemStatement:
Professor X got lost in a maze of an ancient tomb in Egypt. While he was finding the way to escape, he got a message of the tomb builders on the old walls:




The code to open the exit door is the sequence C of n digits formed c1..cn (ci∈[0,9] ).
For every sequence C, combining with the given integers A,B, call:
h0=0
hi=(hi-1×A+ci)  mod B

The smallest sequence C (in lexicological order) satisfying hn=G (where G is a given integer) is the exit code which professor X needs.
Request
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains four space‑separated integers n, A, B, G (1≤n≤12, 10≤A,B≤10^15, 0≤G<B).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a line containing the lexicographically smallest sequence of exactly n decimal digits (0‑9) that satisfies the recurrence and ends with h_n = G.

solution.approach: The recurrence h_i = (h_{i-1}·A + c_i) mod B is linear. After m steps the state can be written as h_{start+m}= (h_{start}·A^m + suffixValue) mod B, where suffixValue is the value obtained by starting from 0 and applying the same recurrence to the m digits of the suffix. Because n≤12 we split the sequence into two halves (k = n/2, m = n‑k). 1️⃣ Forward half: enumerate all 10^k possible prefixes, compute the resulting state h after k digits and store for each state the lexicographically smallest prefix that reaches it (a simple DFS that tries digits 0…9 in order guarantees minimality). 2️⃣ Backward half: pre‑compute powA = A^m mod B. Enumerate all 10^m possible suffixes, simultaneously computing suffixValue. For a suffix we need a prefix state h such that (h·powA + suffixValue) ≡ G (mod B). This is a linear congruence h·powA ≡ (G‑suffixValue) (mod B). Let g = gcd(powA,B). If (G‑suffixValue) mod g ≠ 0 there is no solution for this suffix. Otherwise we divide the equation by g, compute the modular inverse of (powA/g) modulo (B/g) using the extended Euclidean algorithm and obtain the base solution h0. All solutions are h = h0 + t·(B/g) for t = 0…g‑1. For each solution we check whether it appears in the forward map; the first time we find a match we combine the stored prefix with the current suffix. Because prefixes are stored minimal for their state and suffixes are generated in lexicographic order, the first combined string found is the overall lexicographically smallest answer. The algorithm runs in O(10^{n/2} + 10^{n‑n/2}) ≈ O(10^6) operations per test case, easily fast for the given limits.
solution.timeComplexity: O(10^{⌈n/2⌉} + 10^{⌊n/2⌋}) per test case (≈ 2·10^6 for n=12). The dominant operations are modular multiplications using 128‑bit arithmetic and a few gcd/inverse calculations (log B).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(10^{⌈n/2⌉}) to store the forward map (at most 10^6 entries).
